EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
This report presents an independent CSR Impact Assessment 
of Sona BLW Precision Forgings Limited (“Sona Comstar”) 
portfolio of eleven flagship initiatives implemented during 
FY 2025-26.
Sona Comstar's CSR Portfolio has been rated as Exemplary 
using the Bluesky proprietary Process Maturity & Goals 
Achievement (PMGA) Framework borrows from the guidance 
available with Security and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) 
Social Auditing Standards and ISO 26000: Guidance on Social 
Responsibility.
Exemplary Projects have high process maturity and are 
successfully reaching their program goals. These projects 
represent best practices in implementation and impact, 
demonstrating an exemplary model for other projects 
to follow. Thematic areas of Sona Comstar's CSR portfolio 
are aligned with UN Sustainable Development Goals.
The assessment evaluates both:
•	 Process maturity (design, governance, implementation 
quality)
•	 Goal achievement (outputs, outcomes, early impact)
Sona Comstar’s CSR portfolio reflects a mature and 
strategically diversified approach, combining direct 
community interventions with ecosystem-level investments. 
At one end of the spectrum, programs such as sanitation, 
school infrastructure deliver immediate, large-scale human 
impact, addressing fundamental needs of nutrition, health, 
and dignity. At the other end, innovation-led initiatives such 
as FITT and IIMA Ventures operate upstream, shaping future 
industries, technological capabilities, and economic systems.
Between these, education and skilling programs act as 
bridges, translating opportunity into employability and long-
term socio-economic mobility. This layered portfolio design 
enables Sona Comstar to create impact across multiple time 
horizons:
•	 Immediate (health, sanitation)
•	 Medium-term (education, livelihoods)
•	 Long-term (innovation, environment)
Overall, the CSR strategy demonstrates a clear shift from 
compliance-driven philanthropy to strategic, systems-
oriented impact creation, with strong foundations for scale, 
sustainability and long-term value.

SONA COMSTAR PORTFOLIO SNAPSHOT
Sona Comstar’s CSR portfolio addresses immediate human 
development needs while simultaneously strengthening long-
term institutional, economic, and environmental resilience. 
The portfolio is presented below across 6 sectors.
A.	
Projects in Innovation & Entrepreneurship
	
1.	
FITT – IIT Delhi
	
2.	
IIMA Ventures – IIM Ahmedabad
	
Strategic Objective: To strengthen India’s clean mobility 
and deep-tech innovation ecosystem by supporting 
early-stage startups working in electric mobility, energy 
storage, advanced manufacturing, and sustainable 
technologies.
	
Implementing Partner: The interventions are 
implemented through Foundation for Innovation and 
Technology Transfer (FITT), IIT Delhi and IIMA Ventures, 
two leading innovation and incubation platforms 
supporting technology commercialisation, startup 
acceleration, and ecosystem development.
	
Geography: The programs operate across major 
innovation and manufacturing corridors including Delhi, 
Bengaluru, Chennai, Pune, and Ahmedabad.
	
Project Outputs:
	
Under the FITT partnership
•	 16 startups supported cumulatively from FY 2020‑26
•	 Approximately INR 8.9 crore CSR investment towards 
startup funding
•	 Sona Comstar funds disbursement has leveraged 
approximately ₹63.45 crore of external funding (~10x 
leverage) for the start ups
•	 In FY 2025-2026, 900+ startup applications 
processed, 11 short listed and 4 awarded
•	 Startups supported across mobility verticals of 
EVs, hydrogen, motors, batteries, and charging 
technologies.
	
Under IIMA Ventures
•	 7 startups supported from FY 2022-26; 2 startups 
actively scaled in FY 2025-26
•	 Commercialisation support provided to ventures such 
as Ergon Labs and Blinq Mobility;
•	 OEM-linked deployment and market validation 
achieved;
•	 Follow-on funding exceeding USD 4.5 million 
mobilised.
•	 15,000+ km testing done by Ergon Labs and 10,000-
unit OEM-linked supply pipeline
	
Impact Assessment Insight: The portfolio demonstrates 
strong ecosystem-level impact by reducing 
commercialisation barriers for deep-tech startups 
and strengthening India’s indigenous innovation 
capabilities. The interventions have enabled startups 
to transition from prototype validation toward early 
commercialisation while contributing to clean mobility, 
advanced manufacturing, and low-carbon economic 
growth pathways.
B.	
Projects in Skilling & Employability
	
3.	
Tata STRIVE – Chennai
	
4.	
Tata STRIVE – Pune
	
5.	
Tata STRIVE –Faridabad
	
Strategic Objective: To address employability gaps 
among youth by creating industry-aligned skilling 
pathways linked to formal employment, livelihood 
generation, and workforce participation.
	
Implementing Ecosystem: The interventions are 
implemented through Tata STRIVE under the Tata 
Community Initiatives Trust, with strong industry linkages 
and placement-focused vocational training models.
	
Geography: The centres are located across key industrial 
and manufacturing ecosystems including Chennai, Pune, 
and Faridabad, aligned with demand in automotive, 
manufacturing, BFSI, hospitality, and emerging EV 
sectors.
	
Project Outputs
	
At the Chennai centre (FY 2025-2026)
•	 623 youth trained cumulatively across Manufacturing, 
BFSI, and Hospitality of which 426 youth trained 
during FY 2025-26 alone;
•	 Of the 623 youth trained, 486 youth were placed 
during the period FY 2023-2026, 331 youth placed 
during FY 2025-26 of which 264 were women.
•	 Cumulative placement rate of youth is 78%;
•	 Strong female participation evidenced ranging 
between 53% and ~70% in FY 2025-26;
•	 160 women utilised hostel facilities enabling 
participation from rural and mobility-constrained 
backgrounds;

Post-Placement tracking:
•	 For the batch certified in FY 2024-25, approximately 
120 youth (out of 227 youth outreached) reported 
employment at an average monthly salary of INR

rate of approximately 62%,) reported employment at 
an average monthly salary of INR 18,000. This roughly 
translates to an annual income generation of INR 2.85 
Cr (264 × 6months × INR 18,000).
	
The Pune and Faridabad centres are positioned to address 
emerging workforce requirements linked to Industry 4.0, 
EV technologies, and advanced manufacturing in the 
next financial year.
	
Impact Assessment Insight: The interventions 
demonstrate strong alignment between skilling, 
employability, and livelihood outcomes. Beyond 
technical training, the programs contributed to 
increased confidence, workplace readiness, women’s 
workforce participation, and first-generation 
employment opportunities for underserved youth.

C.	
Projects in Education & Learning Systems Strengthening
	
6.	
VIDYA Gurugram
	
7.	
School Infrastructure – Nuh
	
Strategic Objective: To strengthen foundational learning 
ecosystems by improving educational continuity, school 
infrastructure, student engagement, and access to 
enabling learning environments.
	
Implementing Ecosystem: The initiatives are 
implemented through VIDYA and Hindu Shiksha 
Foundation, organisations with strong grassroots 
engagement in underserved educational ecosystems.
	
Geography: The interventions operate in Gurugram 
(Vidya) and Nuh district (Hindu Shiksha Foundation) 
Haryana — regions characterised by educational 
vulnerabilities, infrastructure deficits, and low learning 
continuity among underserved communities.
	
Portfolio Scale & Outputs:
	
Under the VIDYA program
	
1.	
The NIOS Open Schooling Programme (National 
Institute of Open Schooling) implemented at Vidya 
School, Gurugram, supported 100 school dropouts 
in re-entering the formal education system through 
the National Institute of Open Schooling (NIOS) 
framework.
	
	
The program combined classroom instruction with 
digital literacy, life-skills education, exposure visits, 
and entrepreneurship orientation to strengthen 
both academic and employability outcomes. 
Delivered through a team of five teachers along 
with program support staff, the initiative focused on 
enabling learners to complete formal certification 
while improving literacy, numeracy, confidence, and 
future livelihood prospects.
	
	
The students have appeared for the exam in May 26 
and are awaiting results.
	
2.	
The Remedial Support Programme at Vidya School, 
Gurugram, was designed to address learning gaps 
among 660 students from Grades 6 to 12 through 
targeted academic support and personalised 
instruction.
•	 Number of students covered under the 
program: 660
•	 660 students supported under remedial 
education initiatives
•	 Baseline assessment conducted on: Half-Yearly 
Examination (Grades 6-8) and PT-1/First Periodic 
Test (Grades 9-11)
•	 Endline assessment conducted on: Annual 
Examination / Pre-Board II for Grade 10 
(2025- 26)
•	 Percentage of students demonstrating 
improvement in overall test scores: 65%
•	 Average improvement in baseline versus 
endline assessment scores: Approximately 
8–12% improvement in academic performance 
across core subjects
•	 Percentage of students showing improvement 
in English reading and writing skills: 66%
•	 Percentage of students showing improvement 
in Mathematics competency: 75%
•	 Percentage of students progressing from 
below-grade level to grade-appropriate 
competency: 65%
•	 Improvement in average student attendance: 
19%
•	 Reduction in absenteeism/dropout rate: 87%
	
Under the School Infrastructure Project at Nuh
•	 1650 Total number of beneficiaries across 2 schools
•	 Hindu Vidya Niketan 860 children (516 boys &344 
girls)
•	 Hindu Senior Secondary School 790 children (553 
boys & 237 girls)
•	 2 RO water systems installed (one per school)
•	 Solar-powered electricity system installed Hindu 
Vidya Niketan
•	 Science laboratories established (Physics, Chemistry, 
Biology) at Hindu Vidya Niketan
•	 UPS and LAN systems provisioned to support digital 
and uninterrupted operations at Hindu Vidya Niketan
•	 Maintenance systems established (2-year AMC + 
assigned personnel)
	
Impact Assessment Insight: The interventions 
strengthened enabling conditions for learning 
through improved attendance stabilisation, enhanced 
academic engagement, better access to educational 
infrastructure, and stronger parent-school coordination. 
The portfolio demonstrates a shift from access-based 
interventions toward systemic strengthening of 
learning environments.
D.	
Projects in Sanitation & Hygiene
	
8.	
School Sanitation – Tamil Nadu
	
Strategic Objective: To improve hygiene, dignity, and 
sanitation access among government school students 
through sustained sanitation infrastructure and 
maintenance systems.
	
Implementing Ecosystem: The intervention is directly 
supported by Sona Comstar through long-term sanitation 
infrastructure creation and facility maintenance across 
government schools.
	
Geography: The program operates across government 
schools in Tamil Nadu, particularly in semi-urban and 
rural educational ecosystems.
	
Portfolio Scale & Outputs
•	 10 schools supported till date serving 6,670 students;
•	 Girls constituted approximately 58% of beneficiaries;
•	 Long-term sanitation maintenance systems 
operational across schools;
•	 Dedicated restroom infrastructure developed with 
cleaning and waste-disposal mechanisms.
•	 Toilet complex for students constructed at Anaicut 
School, Chennai in FY 2025-26
	
Impact Assessment Insight: The intervention has 
contributed to improved hygiene practices, increased 
toilet utilisation, better menstrual hygiene management, 
and enhanced dignity among students, particularly girls. 
The sustained maintenance model has enabled the 
transition from one-time infrastructure provision to long-
term usability and behavioural reinforcement.
E.	
Projects in Environment & Climate Resilience
	
9.	
Go Green Initiative - Miyawaki Forest- Catch 
Foundation
	
10.	 Water Conservation – Naam Foundation
	
Strategic Objective: To strengthen ecological restoration, 
climate resilience, water security, and sustainable 
environmental stewardship in vulnerable ecosystems.
	
Implementing Ecosystem: The interventions are 
implemented through CATCH Foundation (Manesar, 
Haryana) and Naam Foundation (Pune, Maharashtra) - 
organisations specialising in afforestation, ecological 
restoration, and community-based water conservation.
	
Geography: The projects operate in Manesar (Haryana) 
and Pabal village in Pune district (Maharashtra), both 
environmentally stressed regions facing ecological 
degradation and water vulnerability.
	
Portfolio Scale & Outputs:
	
Under the Miyawaki intervention
•	 In FY 2025-2026, 23,000 saplings planted using 
dense multi-layer Miyawaki plantation methods;
•	 Plantation included approximately 10,000 bamboo 
and 13,000 native species;
•	 Irrigation, soil conditioning, and ecological restoration 
systems established;
•	 Estimated plantation survival rates observed at ~90% 
for native species and ~93% for bamboo plantations;

F.	
Projects in Women Empowerment & Access to

11.	 Ashoka University Scholarships
	
Strategic Objective: To reduce financial barriers 
restricting 	
women’s access to high-quality higher 
education and long-term socio-economic mobility.
	
Implementing Ecosystem: The intervention is 
implemented through Ashoka University under a 
structured scholarship support model for meritorious 
women students with high financial need.
	
Geography: The program is anchored in Sonipat, Haryana, 
with beneficiaries drawn from financially constrained and 
educationally underserved backgrounds across India.
	
Portfolio Scale & Outputs:
•	 5 women scholars supported in FY 2025-2026
•	 The total value of scholarships AY 2025–26 - INR 
47,04,420
•	 100% tuition fee waivers valued at approximately INR 
10.22 lakh per student
•	  80% tuition fee waivers valued at approximately INR 
8.18 lakh per student.
•	 Tuition waiver based on demonstrated financial need 
and academic potential.
•	 Studies across disciplines such as Economics & 
Finance, Psychology, English & Creative Writing, 
International Relations, and Philosophy,
	
Impact Assessment Insight: The intervention 
demonstrates strong additionality, with beneficiaries 
highly dependent on scholarship support for access 
to higher education. The program has enabled greater 
academic participation, expanded career aspirations, 
improved confidence, and strengthened long-term 
pathways toward economic mobility and professional 
advancement for women scholars.
CHAPTER 1 | BACKGROUND
CSR Impact Assessment is a process which is individual to 
a company and depends on factors such as the size of the 
company and its maturity in addressing the expectations of 
the community it operates in. The purpose of the CSR Impact 
Assessment Report is for Sona Comstar to assess the impact of 
the activities of their CSR efforts, strive to maximise the impact 
of their CSR efforts and to identify areas for improvement and 
further engagement with the community.
About Sona Comstar
•	 Sona BLW Precision Forgings Limited (Sona Comstar) 
is one of the world's leading mobility technology 
companies, designing, manufacturing, and supplying 
highly engineered, mission-critical, high-quality, complex 
and bespoke systems and components for global mobility 
OEMs for both electrified and non-electrified powertrain 
segments.
Headquartered in Gurugram, India, Sona Comstar is a leading 
supplier for the fast-growing global EV market, with 35% of its 
automotive revenues from the EV market. It is also a global 
supplier with 12 manufacturing and assembly facilities across 
India, USA, Mexico, and China, and 49% of its sales are for 
international markets. The Company has a diversified revenue 
base across geographies, products, vehicle segments, and 
customers.
With a robust emphasis on research and development 
across its 5 R&D centres, Sona Comstar develops advanced 
drivetrain systems and high-performance motors for electric 
mobility, mmWave radar sensors and perception solutions for 
autonomous and connected applications, and safety-critical 
solutions for railway applications. It aims to be at the forefront 
of shaping the next generation of mobility technology and 
providing sustainable solutions for customer’s evolving needs. 
It aspires to become one of the world’s most respected and 
valuable mobility technology companies for its customers, 
employees and shareholders.
Sona Comstar CSR Policy
Sona Comstar’s CSR interventions consist of six pillars aimed 
to strengthen and reinforce the nation and the society:
1.	
Sona Comstar-Samridh Bharat (Environment and 
Sustainability)
2.	
Sona Comstar-Stree Shakti Bharat (Women 
Empowerment)
3.	
Sona Comstar-Swachh Bharat (Sanitation and Hygiene)
4.	
Sona Comstar-Saksham Bharat (Skill Development and 
Employability)
5.	
Sona Comstar-Swasth Bharat (Health and Nutrition)
6.	
Sona Comstar-Surakshit Bharat (Safety and Social 
Equity)
Sona Comstar has a well-defined and robust governance 
structure to oversee the implementation of the CSR Policy 
and monitoring of CSR projects as per the requirements of 
Section 135 of the Companies Act, - 2013. The CSR Policy of 
Sona Comstar can be accessed at
https://sonacomstar.com/files/policy/corporate-social-
responsibility-policy-policy-K6JBXy.pdf
Scope of CSR Impact Assessment Exercise
•	 To assess the CSR Projects performance as per the 
pre-defined targets defined in the MoU signed with 
Implementing Agency
•	 To assess if the objectives of the projects have been met.
CHAPTER 2 | IMPACT ASSESSMENT 
METHODOLOGY
Assessment Methodology and Strategic Focus
This CSR Impact Assessment evaluates eleven flagship CSR 
initiatives supported by Sona Comstar in FY 2025–26. The 
assessment was guided by Social Auditing Standards (SAS) 
under SEBI and ISO 26000:2018 on Social Responsibility. The 
PMGA framework (Process Maturity & Goals Achievement) 
was used to assess both implementation design and outcome 
achievement.
Evaluation methods included desk reviews, a logic model 
approach, key informant interviews (KIIs), focus group 
discussions (FGDs) and mixed-method data analysis. Projects 
were rated using BlueSky's proprietary scoring method 
to ensure accountability, transparency, and stakeholder-
centric impact. The projects were cumulatively rated as 
Exemplary, indicating high process maturity & significant goal 
achievements,
CSR Impact Assessment Methodology
The impact assessment methodology adheres to the Social 
Auditing Standards (SAS), regulated by the Institute of Social 
Auditors of India under SEBI, and aligns with ISO 26000:2018 
– Guidance on Social Responsibility. This structured approach 
ensures a comprehensive and transparent evaluation, 
incorporating the key methodologies outlined below.
In order to determine the impact of the CSR Projects supported 
by Sona Comstar, this impact assessment applied the PMGA 
framework (Process Maturity & Goals Achievement).
Based on the Theory of Change/ Logic Model and ISO 26000: 
Guidance for Social Responsibility, the Impact assessment of 
the CSR project is based on 2 sets of criterions.
1.	
Process Maturity Score: X Axis scored on 8 process 
criteria with a score range of Min 1- Max 5. It maps 
processes followed in implementing projects to the 
guidance framework available in ISO 26000 & NGRBC 
(principles of Social Responsibility).
2.	
Programme Goal Score: Y Axis scored on criterion of 
achieving the goals of the project agreed with the client 
with a score range of Min 1-Max 5. These criteria are 
specific to the project and maps to the guidance available 
in the Logic Model developed for the project.
EVALUATION FRAMEWORK
Maps the extent to which predefined project objectives as determined in the 
Project’s Logic Model were achieved and reported in terms of Key Metrics.
Project Process  
Maturity
Project Goals 
Achievement
Maps the project design based on the following criteria  -
1.	
Programme Relevance & Significance
2.	
Project Objectives & Scope
3.	
Project Stakeholder Mapping and Identification
4.	
Project Initiation and Risk Assessment of factors influencing the program
5.	
Project Stakeholder engagement & participation,
6.	
Project Monitoring & Evaluation process,
7.	
Project communication & reporting
8.	
Project sustainability.

APPROACH & METHODOLOGY
Desk review
Data 
Collection
Data Analysis
Logic Model
Assessment 
Tools
Reporting
Stakeholder 
Matrix
Sampling 
(Quantitative, 
Qualitative)
Activity
Details
Desk Review
In-depth review of client documents including project progress reports, MOUs with implementing partners, 
project design, monitoring & evaluation systems. This step ensures assessing the process involved in program 
implementation to understand program efficiency.
Logic Model
Developed based on the desk review to map the relationship between inputs, activities, outputs, outcomes, and 
impacts, ensuring a structured evaluation approach.
Stakeholder Matrix
Identifies and categorises key stakeholders based on their influence, accountability, and interest, ensuring 
effective engagement in the assessment process.
Sampling
Qualitative Sample: Purposive sampling was applied for Focus Group Discussions (FGDs) and Key Informant 
Interviews (KIIs) to gather in-depth insights.
Assessment Tools
Key Informant Interviews (KIIs) and Focus Group Discussions (FGDs) to collect qualitative data.
Data Collection
A mixed-method approach to data collection combines quantitative data (measurable outcomes, statistics) with 
qualitative insights (beneficiary experiences, challenges), ensuring a comprehensive impact evaluation.
Data Analysis & 
Reporting
Findings were analysed on thematic coding for qualitative insights. The information so gathered is then 
triangulated to assess the impact of the initiative. 
Reporting
Structured reporting with key impact metrics and recommendations 
STAKEHOLDER MATRIX
A well designed stakeholder matrix ensures that not only 
are the contributors recognised, but the beneficiaries – 
those whose lives are impacted by the initiative are also 
accounted for. Including all relevant parties guarantees a 
comprehensive social impact assessment, reflective of the 
diverse perspectives at play within the initiative's ecosystem.
The 4 main types of stakeholders engaged for the social 
impact assessment were:
•	 Beneficiary Stakeholders – People who directly benefit 
from the project.
•	 Executing Stakeholders (Management) – Implementation 
Partner team who plan and manage the project.
•	 Executing Stakeholders (Field Teams) – Implementation 
Partner team who carry out the project activities on the 
ground.
•	 Oversight (Sponsor) – The organisation that supports, 
funds, and reviews the project.
The sampling approach ensured both breadth and depth in 
assessing the projects across various project locations.
Qualitative Feedback
A purposive sampling approach was used to gain in-
depth insights from key stakeholders, including school 
girls, trainers, school management representatives, and 
government officials. This mixed-method approach ensured 
a comprehensive evaluation of the project by integrating 
empirical data with stakeholder perspectives, strengthening 
the validity of the findings.
Sampling for Stakeholder Feedback & Engagement
•	 30 Key Informant Interviews
•	 7 Focus Group Discussions
CHAPTER 3 | KEY FINDINGS
PROJECT 1 | FOUNDATION FOR INNOVATION AND 
TECHNOLOGY TRANSFER (FITT)
Sona-Comstar | IIT-Delhi Innovation in Mobility Programme
Focus Year | FY 2025-26
CSR Pillar | Samridh Bharat
Environment & Sustainability
Name of Implementing Partner: Foundation for Innovation 
and Technology Transfer (FITT) 
Project Locations: New Delhi, Bengaluru, Chennai, and Pune.
Programme Name 
Innovation in Mobility Programme
Implementing Agency FITT – IIT Delhi
Year of Initiation 
March 25, 2020
Geographic Spread 
Delhi
Programme Type 
Deep-tech startup incubation & acceleration
Focus Domains 
EVs, batteries, motors, hydrogen, energy 
storage
The IIT Delhi Innovation in Mobility Programme is a strategic 
CSR intervention anchored in enabling India’s transition 
toward:
•	 Low-carbon mobility systems
•	 Energy-efficient technologies
•	 Circular and clean manufacturing ecosystems
It represents a shift from direct beneficiary CSR models 
to ecosystem-level transformation, targeting upstream 
innovation in:
•	 Electric mobility systems
•	 Energy storage technologies
•	 Advanced materials
•	 Clean manufacturing
About Implementing Partner
The Foundation for Innovation and Technology Transfer 
(FITT), the industry interface organisation of the Indian 
Institute of Technology (IIT) Delhi, was established to foster 
the commercialisation of science and technology innovations. 
With a core mission to bridge the gap between academia and 
industry, FITT plays a crucial role in translating cutting-edge 
research into real-world applications by offering targeted 
support to startups, incubators, and technology-driven 
enterprises. Over the years, it has emerged as a key enabler 
of India’s innovation ecosystem, providing incubation support, 
mentorship, funding facilitation, and access to the intellectual 
and infrastructural resources of IIT Delhi.
Recognising the importance of innovation, the Government of 
India has introduced several policies, grants, and partnerships 
to encourage the development of Indian intellectual property 
and foster the growth of technology startups from concept 
to commercialisation. Despite this support, there remains 
a critical need for private sector involvement to provide 
sustained funding and mentorship for such innovations.
Aligned with this national vision, Sona Comstar, a strong 
proponent of intellectual property and innovation, has 
partnered with FITT, IIT Delhi to support startups in the safe, 
convenient, and eco-friendly mobility sector. This programme 
creates a collaborative platform that nurtures innovation 
and contributes meaningfully to India’s rapidly evolving 
technological landscape.
Project Overview
The programme has transitioned from early-stage R&D 
support in 2020 to ecosystem-building platform in 2025 
(including covid years). Over the years it has supported 
hardware-intensive startups, which typically face:
•	 Long gestation cycles
•	 High capital intensity
•	 High failure rates (80–90% at pre-seed stage) programme 
Description
The programme functions as a multi-dimensional innovation 
pipeline, combining:
•	 Capital infusion (grants)
•	 Technical incubation
•	 Market linkage
•	 Institutional credibility (IIT Delhi ecosystem)
Logic Model
Inputs
•	
H 8.9 crore CSR funding deployed towards startup grants
•	
IIT Delhi R&D infrastructure and labs
•	
Industry mentoring from Sona Comstar
Activities
•	 National-level startup scouting
•	 Structured evaluation (tech + business + scalability)
•	 Milestone-based grant disbursement
•	 Continuous mentoring, quarterly reviews
•	 Industry validation and investor engagement

Sona Comstar Annual Report 2025-26
155
Corporate

reports
Financial 
statements
Outputs
•	 16 startups supported cumulatively - from FY 2020 - 
FY 2026
•	 900 applications processed (FY 25-26)
•	 11 startups shortlisted, 4 funded (FY 25-26)
•	 Multiple prototypes and IP filings
Outcomes
•	 Near zero startup failure rate
•	 H 63.45 crore external funding mobilised (~10x leverage)
•	 Startups achieving TRL advancement* (6 → 9)
•	 Market-ready products and early commercialisation
Impact
•	 Strengthened clean mobility innovation ecosystem
•	 Reduced technology import dependence
•	 Enabled low-carbon economic growth pathways
*Technology Readiness Levels (TRL) are a 9-level scale used to assess the 
maturity of a technology, ranging from initial research (TRL 1) to proven, 
market-ready deployment (TRL 9). Primarily developed by NASA, it helps 
organisations measure development progress and estimate feasibility 
for engineering projects, commonly used in sectors like engineering, 
manufacturing, and defense
Project Objectives
•	 Support the development and commercialisation of clean 
mobility technologies
•	 Enable deep-tech startups to move from prototype to 
market
•	 Strengthen indigenous R&D and manufacturing capabilities
The initiative specifically targets early-stage startups 
engaged in research, prototyping, and market validation in the 
e-mobility sector—a segment where startups often struggle 
due to limited access to capital, laboratories, and expert 
guidance. Through this partnership, the programme aims 
to empower these innovators and accelerate their journey 
toward impactful and scalable solutions.
Project Activities
The project followed a structured approach to identify and 
support high-potential startups across the country. It began 
with nationwide outreach through academic institutions, 
startup ecosystems, and digital campaigns, resulting in 900 
applications in FY 25 -26
A rigorous multi-stage evaluation process was implemented, 
including eligibility checks, technical screening, and expert 
panel reviews. The evaluation committee comprised IIT Delhi 
experts, industry leaders, investors, and legal professionals, 
ensuring a credible and comprehensive selection.
The programme provided grant-based financial support, 
with 80% of funds allocated directly to startups, while 
the remainder supported ecosystem development and 
programme operations at FIIT.
Selected startups benefited from incubation and technical 
support, including access to IIT labs, testing facilities, and 
research mentorship, enabling technology refinement and 
product development.
Finally, the initiative enabled market access and 
commercialisation through investor pitch events, industry 
pilots, and global exposure opportunities, helping startups 
scale and achieve market readiness.
List of Beneficiaries over the Years + Project Outputs
Beneficiary Startups (Cumulative Portfolio)
•	 Total Direct Beneficiaries: 16 startups
Representative Portfolio (Sector-wise)
 Electric Mobility
•	 InGO Electric → 360+ vehicles sold
•	 Creatara Mobility → EV 2-wheelers launched
Powertrain & Motors
•	 Quanteon Powertrains → 94% motor efficiency
•	 Motorama → rare-earth-free motors
Energy Storage & Batteries
•	 Octarange → AI-driven battery systems
•	 Aatral → Sodium-ion batteries
Advanced Materials & Hydrogen
•	 Grokalp H2CNT → CNT + hydrogen production
•	 Ares Systems → membrane-free hydrogen electrolysis
Charging Infrastructure
•	 Varnika Energy (EV Recharge) → mobile EV charging

Impact Assessment
The programme has led to significant behavioural and 
systemic shifts, as evidenced by key informant interviews 
(KIIs) and stakeholder insights. Notably, it has contributed to 
reduced risk aversion in deep-tech investments and fostered 
greater confidence in indigenous innovation. The initiative 
has also strengthened collaboration between startups 
and industry, while supporting the growth of the Make in 
India manufacturing ecosystem, creating a more enabling 
environment for innovation-led development.
In terms of cumulative impact, the programme has delivered 
results across multiple time horizons. In the short term, it 
has facilitated prototype development, advancement in 
Technology Readiness Levels (TRL), and the creation of 
intellectual property (IP). Moving into the medium term, 
startups have achieved market entry, begun generating 
revenue, and secured follow-on funding, indicating growing 
commercial viability.
Over the long term, the programme is expected to generate 
substantial economic, environmental, and systemic benefits. 
Economically, it is supporting the emergence of high-growth 
startups contributing to GDP and enabling the creation of 
innovation-driven industries.
Environmentally, it is accelerating decarbonisation efforts 
and reducing dependence on fossil fuels. Systemically, the 
initiative is helping to institutionalise a robust innovation 
ecosystem and reduce early-stage startup mortality, ensuring 
sustained impact and long-term resilience.
The programme demonstrates high economic multiplier 
effects, converting early-stage grants into large-scale private 
capital inflows.
Programme Stakeholders noted that:
“Milestone-based funding helps manage the high failure 
rates associated with early-stage startups by ensuring 
accountability and enabling steady progress.”
“Additionally, access to shared infrastructure significantly 
reduces capital barriers, allowing startups to innovate more 
efficiently without heavy upfront investments.”
PROJECT 2 | CENTRE FOR INNOVATION 
INCUBATION AND ENTREPRENEURSHIP BUILDING 
(CIIE INITIATIVES, IIMA VENTURES )
Focus Year: FY 2025–26
CSR Pillar: Samridh Bharat
Environment & Sustainability
About Implementing partner
IIMA Ventures, the innovation and entrepreneurship centre 
at the Indian Institute of Management Ahmedabad, is one 
of India’s leading startup incubators focused on supporting 
high-impact and technology-driven enterprises. Established 
to nurture entrepreneurship and innovation, the organisation 
provides early-stage startups with incubation support, seed 
funding, mentoring, market access, and strategic partnerships 
across sectors such as mobility, climate-tech, healthcare, and 
advanced manufacturing.
Recognising the importance of indigenous innovation 
and deep-tech entrepreneurship in driving sustainable 
economic growth, Sona Comstar partnered with CIIE-IIMA 
Ventures to support startups working in clean mobility, 
advanced manufacturing, and future technologies. Through 
this collaboration, the programme aims to reduce barriers 
faced by early-stage startups, facilitate access to capital and 
industry networks, and contribute to building a self-reliant and 
innovation-led economy aligned with national priorities such 
as Atmanirbhar Bharat.
Project Overview
The Sona Comstar-supported engagement with IIMA Ventures 
is a strategic CSR initiative aimed at catalysing deep-tech 
entrepreneurship in India. The programme provides early-
stage capital, incubation, and ecosystem access to startups 
operating in high-impact sectors such as climate tech, mobility, 
aerospace, and healthcare.
In FY 2025–26, the initiative has matured into a portfolio-
level intervention, supporting startups transitioning from pilot 
validation to early commercialisation, particularly in electric 
mobility and advanced powertrain technologies.
Project Objectives
Primary Objectives of IIMA Ventures
•	 Enable the commercial readiness of deep-tech startups
•	 Bridge the “valley of death” in pre-seed innovation
•	 Generate measurable ESG-aligned impact (social, 
environmental, and economic)
•	 Strengthen India’s deep-tech manufacturing and innovation 
ecosystem
FY 2025–26 Strategic Focus
•	 Scaling mobility and clean-tech solutions
•	 Supporting IP-led and hardware-driven startups
•	 Facilitating market access and enterprise partnerships
Project Activities
Core Activities (Cumulative)
•	 Pre-seed funding support (INR 50 lakh per startup)
•	 Incubation, mentoring, and milestone tracking
•	 Access to R&D infrastructure, partner institutes, and 
institutional networks
•	 Investor connect and fundraising support
FY 2025–26 Focused Activities
•	 Commercial pilot enablement (Ergon Labs, Blinq Mobility)
•	 OEM partnerships and supply agreements
•	 IP development and validation support through customer 
and investor connects
•	 Scaling production readiness and deployment models
Illustrative Outcomes
•	 Ergon Labs conducted 15,000+ km of testing and 500+ 
charge cycles
•	 A supply agreement was signed for 10,000 units with a 
major OEM
•	 Blinq Mobility progressed through validation under the 
Maruti Innovation programme

Project Outputs
Key Outputs (FY 2025–26 Focus)

and Ergon Labs)
•	 Transition from pilot to early commercialisation stage
•	 Supply chain integration initiated with OEMs
•	 IP filings and product validation milestones achieved
Cumulative Outputs
•	 7 startups supported across FY 2022–26
•	 Significant follow-on funding mobilised (>$4.5M 
cumulative); capital deployed in supported startups has 
unlocked access to a larger pool of funding from other 
ecosystem stakeholders
•	 Global partnerships and pilot deployments across sectors
Startups Supported (Portfolio Perspective)
FY 2025–26 Startups (Primary Focus)
Blinq Mobility
Building affordable electric cars
•	 Lower cost per km, leading to higher driver income potential 
(~47% earnings on fuel/EMI savings)
•	 Replaces high-usage ICE taxis, resulting in significant CO₂ 
reduction over the vehicle lifecycle
•	 Particularly suited for dense, price-sensitive cities in 
Southeast Asia and Latin America
•	 Validation through the Maruti Zonal programme
Ergon Labs
Powertrain technologies for light electric vehicles
•	 Improved reliability of on-board systems, reducing 
downtime and enhancing income stability for last-mile 
workers
•	 Enhances EV efficiency (↑15% range, ↓30% weight, ↓30% 
cost)
•	 Applicable across EV OEMs
•	 Supply agreement signed with OSM, the fifth-largest three-
wheeler OEM, to deliver 10,000 units over the next year
Impact Assessment
1.	
Growth and Sustainability
	
The portfolio demonstrates strong commercial 
momentum, with startups progressing from pilots to 
revenue-generating stages. Notable indicators include:
•	 Ergon Labs’ 10,000-unit order pipeline with targeted 
profitability
•	 Renkube’s ~$1.5M pilot and ongoing funding 
conversations
•	 Established IP assets (IoTA, Guerin) and capital-
efficient models (Blinq Mobility)
	
Overall, sustainability is structurally embedded through 
scalable business models, IP creation, and early market 
validation.
2.	
Aspirations and Innovation Trajectory
	
Startups exhibit ambitious, long-term innovation 
roadmaps:
•	 Guerin: Space technologies evolving toward 
interplanetary applications
•	 Nabhdrishti: Advancing toward hybrid-electric 
aviation with potential to serve a global customer base
•	 Ergon Labs: Positioning as a core EV powertrain layer 
provider
•	 Hydrovert: Targeting commercial hydrogen mobility 
by 2027
	
The portfolio is collectively transitioning from proof-of-
concept to certification and commercialisation, marking 
a critical scale-up phase.
3.	
Contribution to the Ecosystem
	
The startups are generating system-level impact beyond 
individual ventures:
•	 Strengthening domestic supply chains and 
manufacturing capabilities
•	 Driving job creation in high-skill R&D roles
•	 Enabling public health solutions (IoTA) and agri-
income diversification (Renkube)
	
This contributes to building a self-reliant, innovation-
driven Indian economy.
4.	
Framing Impact (Deep-Tech Lens)
	
Impact operates at two levels:
•	 Startup level: Technical validation and market fit (e.g., 
IoTA’s clinical accuracy)
•	 Ecosystem level: Risk reduction through incubation, 
partnerships, and shared infrastructure
	
By bridging these levels, IIMA Ventures effectively 
addresses the “valley of death” in deep-tech, linking 
innovation outcomes to national priorities such as 
Atmanirbhar Bharat.
	
Programme Stakeholders noted that: The programme:
•	 Enabled early validation and commercialisation 
readiness
•	 Catalysed follow-on funding and partnerships
•	 Supported team expansion and capability building
	
With FY 2025–26 marking a critical inflection toward 
commercialisation, the initiative is well positioned 
to deliver long-term, scalable ESG impact while 
strengthening India’s position in the global innovation 
landscape.
PROJECT 3 | SONACOMSTAR – TATA STRIVE SKILL 
DEVELOPMENT CENTRE, CHENNAI
CSR Pillar: Saksham Bharat programme
Name of Implementing Partner: Tata Strive
Project Locations: Chennai, Faridabad, Pune
About Implementing partner
Tata STRIVE, an initiative of the Tata Community Initiatives 
Trust, is a national skill development programme established 
to bridge the gap between education and employability among 
Indian youth. The organisation focuses on delivering industry-
aligned vocational training, behavioural development, and 
placement-linked skilling programmes across sectors such 
as manufacturing, BFSI, hospitality, automotive, and emerging 
technologies.
With a vision to create sustainable employment opportunities 
for underserved youth, particularly women and first-generation 
learners, Tata STRIVE combines technical training with soft 
skills, counselling, and placement facilitation. Sona Comstar’s 
partnership with Tata STRIVE supports the establishment 
and strengthening of skill development centres across 
Chennai, Pune, and Faridabad, enabling youth to access 
industry-relevant training and formal employment pathways. 
The collaboration contributes to enhancing employability, 
workforce participation, and long-term socio-economic 
mobility among aspiring youth.
Project Overview
The Tata STRIVE Skill Development Centre in Chennai 
represents a structured and outcome-oriented intervention 
under the CSR mandate of livelihood generation and skill 
development. Implemented by Tata STRIVE under the Tata 
Community Initiatives Trust, the programme addresses 
the persistent gap between education and employability 
among youth, particularly those from rural and underserved 
backgrounds.
The programme integrates industry-aligned training, 
behavioural development, and placement facilitation into 
a single cohesive model. At the time of assessment (March 
2026), it reflects a mature skilling ecosystem, with strong 
evidence of both scale and outcomes. A total of 623 students 
have completed and trained (FY 2023-2026) demonstrating 
significant outreach and operational capacity.

Project Objectives
The intervention is anchored in creating a skilled, employable, 
and industry-aligned workforce. It delivers certified vocational 
training across key sectors such as Manufacturing (CNC 
Machine Technician), BFSI, (Banking, Financial Services & 
Insurance )and Hospitality (Front Office Associate), while 
enhancing soft skills, communication, and workplace 
readiness.
The short term skills training courses offered is aligned to the 
National Skills Qualifications Framework (NSQF) as per the 
details provided below.
A strong focus on women’s inclusion is embedded in the 
programme, enabling female participation in non-traditional 
roles and supporting entry into the formal workforce.
The program aims to:
•	 Train youth in industry-relevant vocational skills
•	 Facilitate employment and income generation
•	 Promote women’s inclusion in the workforce
•	 Build communication, confidence, and workplace readiness
•	 Deliver certified training aligned with NSDC standards

Women Participation 2025-26
The program follows a sequenced, process-driven 
implementation model, beginning with mobilisation and 
ending with placement and post-placement tracking.
•	 Community-driven mobilisation (10–15 days) ensures 
outreach to rural and semi-urban youth
•	 Psychometric assessments align candidates with suitable 
career paths
•	 Multi-level counselling (students + parents) addresses 
socio-cultural barriers, especially for women
•	 Training integrates technical instruction with behavioural 
development through interactive methods
•	 Curriculum is co-developed with industry and aligned with 
QP’S AS PER NSQF standards
•	 Assessments through TCS iON (third-party platform) 
enhance credibility
•	 A dedicated placement ecosystem connects students to 
employers
Overall Training Reach and Inclusion
•	 426 students trained in FY 2025–26 (till March 2026)
•	 Female participation remains strong across FY 2025–26 
cohorts, ranging between 53% and 70%. This indicates a 
significant shift toward gender inclusion, particularly in 
non-traditional sectors.
Hostel as an Enabler of Access
A defining feature of the program is the hostel facility, 
which enables participation from rural and distant regions, 
especially for women.
•	 160 women (~38%) have utilised hostel facilities in 
FY 2025‑26 Course-wise dependency:
•	 BFSI → highest hostel usage
•	 FOA → strong dependency (especially rural women)
•	 CNC → relatively lower (more local students)
The hostel plays a critical role in enabling mobility, 
transforming access into participation, particularly for women.
Project Outputs
Placement Performance (FY 2025-26):
The placement was spread across 30 BFSI Companies, 14 
CNC companies , 10 FOA Companies (FOA) demonstrating 
diversified employment pathways and strong industry linkage.
Some notable companies are:
•	 Manufacturing: Delphi-TVS, Wheels India
•	 BFSI: BankBazaar, PolicyBazaar
•	 Hospitality: Taj Connemara, ITC Hotels
PLACEMENT DETAILS FOR 2025-26
FY 2025-26

PLACEMENT DETAILS CUMULATIVE
FY 2023-26

623
TOTAL PLACED

Cumulatively placement percentage of about 78%
Post-Placement Tracking & Social Return on Investment 
(SROI)
Tata STRIVE tracks the employment outcomes of all certified 
youth for a period of one year from the date of certification. 
Connect attempts are made at 3, 6, 9, and 12 month intervals 
by the Tata STRIVE call centre team. In practice, the team is 
able to reach 30–40% of the total certified youth at any given 
interval — not for lack of effort, but due to factors outside the 
programme's control: changed phone numbers, workplace 
policies restricting phone use during working hours, travel, 
and similar reasons.
For the batch certified in 2024–25, the team made 227 
outreach instances (non-exclusive), of which 131 youth 
confirmed they were employed — roughly 60% of those 
reached. Extrapolating this to the full cohort, approximately 
120 youth can be considered employed. At an average 
monthly salary of INR 18,500, this translates to an annual 
income generation of INR 2.66 Cr (120 × 12 × 18,500).
For 2025–26, outreach was made across 313 instances (non-
exclusive), with 193 youth reporting active employment — a 
working rate of approximately 62%, at an average monthly 
salary of INR 18,000. On that basis, the income generated over 
6 months stands at INR 2.85 Cr (264 × 6 × 18,000).
Impact Assessment
At the current stage, the Tata STRIVE Skill Development 
Centre demonstrates strong outcome-level impact across 
employability, workforce participation, and behavioural 
transformation among youth beneficiaries.
While long-term livelihood sustainability and career 
progression outcomes will evolve over time, the intervention 
has already generated measurable improvements in 
employment readiness and access to formal job opportunities.
The significant outcome to date is the successful transition of 
youth from education or unemployment into structured skilling 
and employment pathways.
This transformation has resulted in:
•	 Improved employability and workplace readiness
•	 Increased participation of women in vocational and 
technical training
•	 Enhanced confidence, communication, and professional 
behaviour among students
•	 Greater awareness of industry-oriented career 
opportunities
Stakeholder feedback highlights the hostel facility as a critical 
enabler for women’s participation, especially for students from 
rural and mobility-constrained backgrounds.
STAKEHOLDER INSIGHTS
Centre Manager
•	 Strong performance in placements and gender inclusion
•	 Key challenge: mobilising women for technical roles
Trainer
•	 Major gap at entry: communication skills
•	 Strong post-training improvement
•	 Continuous tracking for one year
Students
•	 High satisfaction with curriculum and trainers
•	 Initial hostel adjustment challenges
•	 Strong confidence gains
FGDs
•	 Training is “interactive and different from college”
•	 Confidence and independence are key outcomes
•	 Travel and social barriers remain challenges

PROJECT 4 | PUNE – TATA STRIVE SKILL CENTRE 
(MAHARASHTRA)
Pune, as a leading automotive, manufacturing, and emerging 
technology hub, represents a high-potential ecosystem for 
advanced skill development aligned with Industry 4.0 trends. 
The region is characterised by strong demand for skilled 
professionals in automotive manufacturing, CNC machining, 
mechatronics, and increasingly in EV, automation, and digital 
services. Unlike Faridabad, Pune reflects relatively higher 
alignment between youth aspirations and industry demand, 
particularly in modern, technology-driven roles. However, 
gaps persist in terms of practical, job-ready skills and industry 
exposure, especially among first-time entrants into the 
workforce.
The Tata STRIVE model in Pune is therefore positioned to 
strengthen this ecosystem by delivering industry-aligned, 
future-ready training programs, combining technical skills with 
behavioural and workplace readiness modules. With its strong 
industry linkages and placement-driven approach, the centre 
can act as a catalyst for creating a skilled workforce that meets 
both current industrial needs and emerging technological 
transitions, particularly in EVs, automation, and advanced 
manufacturing.
The centre is ready infrastructurally and will resume the 
training activities soon
PROJECT 5 | FARIDABAD – TATA STRIVE SKILL 
CENTRE (HARYANA)
Faridabad, a major industrial hub in the NCR region, with 
over 6,000 MSMEs and 250+ large industries, presents a 
strong demand for skilled manpower across manufacturing, 
automotive, and electrical sectors.
The assessment highlights a critical paradox: while industries 
face acute skill shortages in roles such as CNC operators, 
electricians, welders, and assembly technicians, local 
youth demonstrate low aspiration for physically intensive 
manufacturing jobs, preferring sectors such as automotive 
(especially EV), BFSI, and digital services.
This demand–aspiration mismatch has led to a heavy 
dependence on migrant labour from neighbouring states. 
Additionally, existing infrastructure such as Government 
ITIs remains underutilised, with only 50–55% enrolment, 
indicating a disconnect between traditional skilling models 
and youth expectations.
In this context, the proposed Tata STRIVE centre is strategically 
positioned to bridge both the industry demand–supply gap 
and aspiration mismatch by offering short-term, job-oriented, 
apprenticeship-integrated courses in CNC, electrician, and 
EV technologies.
This is further strengthened by the inclusion of service-sector 
programs and hostel facilities, which will enhance accessibility, 
mobility, and retention, particularly for underserved youth.
The centre is ready infrastructurally and will resume the 
training activities soon.
PROJECT 6 | SUPPORTING BASIC EDUCATION IN 
PARTNERSHIP WITH VIDYA (VIDYA INTEGRATED 
DEVELOPMENT FOR YOUTH AND ADULTS)
CSR Pillar: Saksham Bharat programme
Name of Implementing Partner: Vidya Integrated 
Development for Youth and Adults (VIDYA)
Project Location: Gurugram
About the Implementing Partner
Vidya Integrated Development for Youth and Adults (VIDYA) 
is a not-for-profit organisation dedicated to empowering 
underserved children, youth, and women through education 
and skill development initiatives. Established with a vision 
to enable equitable access to learning opportunities, VIDYA 
focuses on bridging educational gaps and supporting 
individuals to achieve their full potential. The organisation 
works across multiple programs that combine academic 
support, life skills, and vocational training, fostering holistic 
development and long-term self-reliance.
Project Overview
Vidya implemented 2 projects :
1.	
The National Institute of Open Schooling (NIOS) is 
India’s largest open and distance learning institution, 
established to provide flexible and inclusive educational 
opportunities for learners who are unable to continue 
formal schooling due to social, economic, or personal 
constraints. NIOS conducts public examinations twice 
annually, typically in April–May and October–November, 
while also offering an On-Demand Examination System 
for eligible learners.
	
The intervention covered 100 learners across Open 
Basic Education (OBE), Class 10, and Class 12 levels 
during the November 2025 to April 2026 period. 
The program combined classroom instruction with 
digital literacy, life-skills education, exposure visits, 
and entrepreneurship orientation to strengthen both 
academic and employability outcomes. Delivered 
through a team of five teachers along with program 
support staff, the initiative focused on enabling learners 
to complete formal certification while improving literacy, 
numeracy, confidence, and future livelihood prospects.
2.	
The Remedial Support programme at Vidya School, 
Gurugram, was designed to address learning gaps 
among 660 students from Grades 6 to 12 through 
targeted academic support and personalised instruction. 
Implemented between November 2025 and February 
2026, the intervention provided subject-specific remedial 
classes across core disciplines including Mathematics, 
Science, English, Accounts, and Economics through a 
team of over 20 teachers. The program adopted small-
group teaching methods, regular assessments, exam 
preparation support, and teacher-parent feedback 
mechanisms to strengthen academic performance, 
classroom participation, confidence, and study skills. The 
intervention also aimed to reduce the risk of academic 
underperformance and school dropouts by providing 
focused and continuous learning support aligned with 
mainstream school curriculum requirements.
Project Objectives
The project operates at two interconnected levels:
Student-Level Objectives:
•	 Strengthen foundational competencies in Math, Science, 
and English
•	 Reduce dropout risk and improve continuity
•	 Facilitate re-entry through NIOS pathways
•	 Build confidence, participation, and disciplined study habits
System-Level Objectives:
•	 Reduce reliance on unregulated private tuition ecosystems
•	 Reinforce trust in formal schooling systems
•	 Enable peer-driven, self-sustaining learning environments
This reflects a shift from short-term academic correction to 
long-term behavioural and institutional transformation.
Project Activities
The interventions follows a multi-layered, integrated model 
combining academic, behavioural, and ecosystem-level 
strategies:
•	 The academic layer delivers targeted remedial instruction 
based on continuous diagnostic assessments, ensuring 
that teaching is adaptive rather than standardised.
•	 The monitoring layer tracks attendance daily using an 
early-warning system. A key operational insight is the “2-
day absence rule,” which triggers immediate intervention. 
Approximately 80% of attendance disruptions are 
resolved at this early stage, largely through direct parent 
engagement.
•	 The engagement layer strengthens the ecosystem around 
the student, with regular teacher-parent communication 
and home visits for high-risk cases, ensuring that barriers 
outside the classroom are addressed.
•	 The assessment layer introduces rigor through topic-wise 
quizzes and mock exams, enabling differentiation between 
conceptual and analytical gaps - an important distinction 
often missed in traditional systems.
•	 The pathway layer integrates NIOS as a formal mechanism 
for certification and re-entry, ensuring that students who 
have disengaged are not permanently excluded from the 
education system.
This architecture ensures adaptive learning, strong monitoring, 
and systemic student support.
<!-- page 83 -->
162
Sona Comstar Annual Report 2025-26
163
Corporate 
overview 
Statutory 
reports
Financial 
statements
Project Outputs
The two program’s activities including diagnostic testing, 
remedial sessions, continuous assessments, attendance 
tracking, and parental engagement translate into strong and 
measurable outputs.
In addition to examination preparation and academic support, 
the NIOS programme integrated digital literacy, spoken 
English, career guidance, life-skills training, health and 
wellness awareness, and counselling to strengthen learner 
confidence and future readiness. The intervention also 
included workshops, exposure activities, and continuous 
mentoring to support students in completing formal education 
and progressing towards higher education, vocational training, 
entrepreneurship, or employment.
Impact Assessment
The impact of both the Vidya Project is best understood 
through its causal pathways, validated through stakeholder 
interactions (KIIs and FGDs).
•	 targeted remediation leads to conceptual clarity. As 
students begin to understand core subjects, their fear 
reduces, test scores improve, and confidence increases.
•	 Improved teaching quality and engaging pedagogy drive 
intrinsic motivation, reflected in sustained attendance and 
negligible dropout levels.
•	 Continuous assessment and teacher support foster 
disciplined study habits, with students reportedly studying 
for extended hours often exceeding six hours per day.
•	 At the household level, reduced dependence on private 
tuition lowers financial burden and increases parental trust 
in the school system, strengthening educational continuity.
Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) For Vidya Remedial 
Support programme
•	 Number of students covered under the program: 660
•	 Baseline assessment conducted on: Half-Yearly 
Examination (Grades 6–8) and PT-1/First Periodic Test 
(Grades 9–11)
•	 Endline assessment conducted on: Annual Examination / 
Pre-Board II for Grade 10 (2025–26)
•	 Percentage of students demonstrating improvement in 
overall test scores: 65%
•	 Average improvement in baseline versus endline 
assessment scores: Approximately 8–12% improvement 
in academic performance across core subjects
•	 Percentage of students showing improvement in English 
reading and writing skills: 66%
•	 Percentage of students showing improvement in 
Mathematics competency: 75%
•	 Percentage of students progressing from below-grade 
level to grade-appropriate competency: 65%
•	 Improvement in average student attendance: 19%
•	 Reduction in absenteeism/dropout rate: 87%
Learning has shifted from fear-driven to confidence-driven, 
and classrooms have evolved from passive environments 
to spaces of active participation. Students exhibit increased 
agency, taking ownership of their learning, while families 
report reduced conflict around education and greater trust 
in schools.
The project also creates a cost substitution effect, reducing 
reliance on private tuition - an important relief for low - income 
households. Over time, improved educational continuity 
enhances employability prospects, contributing to upward 
economic mobility.
At a structural level, the intervention addresses systemic gaps 
in public education delivery by:
•	 Replacing fragmented tuition ecosystems
•	 Reinforcing schools as primary learning institutions
•	 Enabling peer-led learning cultures
This systemic correction represents one of the program’s most 
critical contributions.
Stakeholder Voices
•	 “Students now study for more than six hours.” (Teacher)
•	 “Earlier we had to force them to study; now they do it 
themselves.” (Parent)
•	 “We are confident about passing our exams.” (NIOS 
Student)
•	 “Attendance issues are resolved early through strong 
coordination.” (programme Head)

PROJECT 7 | BUILDING OF INFRASTRUCTURE IN 
SCHOOLS IN NUH, HARYANA
CSR Pillar: Saksham Bharat Programme
Name of Implementing Partner: Hindu Shikshah Foundation
Project Locations: Manesar, Gurugram
About Implementing Partner
The intervention is anchored in Quality Education, with strong 
linkages to Student Health & Hygiene and Clean Energy 
Access. By addressing foundational infrastructure gaps, the 
project adopts an integrated CSR approach that enables both 
learning outcomes and well-being improvements.
The project has been implemented across two schools in Nuh 
district:
•	 Hindu Vidya Niketan - 860 students (516 boys and 344 
girls)
•	 Hindi Senior Secondary School -790 students (553 boys 
& 235 girls)
Nuh (Mewat) is widely recognised as an educationally 
and socio-economically backward district in Haryana, 
characterised by low literacy levels, gender disparities in 
education, and weak public infrastructure. The region also 
faces chronic infrastructure deficits, including unreliable 
electricity and limited access to safe drinking water, which 
directly impact schooling outcomes.
The intervention was completed in February 2026, with 
approximately one month elapsed since implementation. 
Therefore, this assessment focuses on validated outputs and 
early adoption signals, rather than long-term impact.
Scale of Intervention
•	 2 schools covered
•	 2 RO water systems installed (one per school)
•	 Solar-powered electricity system installed Hindu Vidya 
Niketan
•	 Science laboratories established (Physics, Chemistry, 
Biology) at Hindu Vidya Niketan
•	 UPS and LAN systems provisioned to support digital and 
uninterrupted operations at Hindu Vidya Niketan
•	 Maintenance systems established (2-year AMC + assigned 
personnel)
Project Overview
The school infrastructure intervention in Nuh, Haryana is 
a comprehensive education-support initiative aimed at 
improving foundational learning environments in underserved 
schools. Implemented in partnership with Hindu Shiksha 
Foundation, the project addresses critical infrastructure gaps 
related to safe drinking water, electricity, science education, 
and digital enablement.
Situated in Nuh, one of Haryana’s most educationally 
vulnerable regions, the project seeks to create enabling 
conditions for improved student participation, comfort, and 
academic engagement. By strengthening essential school 
infrastructure, the initiative contributes to enhancing the 
quality of education and building more resilient and student-
friendly learning ecosystems.
Project Objectives
The intervention was designed to address critical bottlenecks 
affecting the learning environment, with the following 
objectives:
•	 Provide safe and reliable drinking water within school 
premises
•	 Ensure continuous electricity supply for classrooms and 
facilities
<!-- page 84 -->
164
Sona Comstar Annual Report 2025-26
165
Corporate 
overview 
Statutory 
reports
Financial 
statements
•	 Enable practical, lab-based science education
•	 Strengthen institutional readiness for higher secondary 
education delivery
Project Activities
Prior to the intervention, both schools faced systemic 
infrastructure constraints:
•	 Poor quality and unreliable access to drinking water
•	 Frequent electricity disruptions causing discomfort and 
reduced learning efficiency
•	 Absence of laboratory infrastructure, limiting learning to 
theoretical instruction
The project addressed these challenges through a bundled 
infrastructure approach, combining:
•	 Water (RO systems)
•	 Energy (solar power + UPS backup)
•	 Learning infrastructure (labs)
•	 Digital enablement (LAN connectivity)
1.	
This integrated model ensures that improvements are 
mutually reinforcing, enabling a transition from basic 
functionality to a more enabling learning ecosystem.
2.	
The intervention was implemented through four key 
activity streams:
3.	
Water Infrastructure: Installation of 2 RO water purification 
systems, ensuring safe and accessible drinking water for 
all students.
4.	
Energy Infrastructure: Deployment of a solar-powered 
electricity system, supplemented by UPS systems, 
ensuring uninterrupted power supply at Hindu Vidya 
Niketan
5.	
Academic Infrastructure: Establishment of three fully 
equipped science laboratories, enabling practical and 
experiential learning at Hindu Vidya Niketan
6.	
Digital & Operational Enablement: Provision of UPS and 
LAN systems, supporting:
•	 Continuous operation of digital tools
•	 Improved administrative efficiency
•	 Future integration of smart learning systems
In parallel, institutional mechanisms (AMC contracts and staff 
responsibilities) were established to ensure sustainability.
Project Outputs
At the time of assessment, all outputs have been successfully 
delivered and are operational.
Both schools now have functional RO systems, ensuring 
safe drinking water access. In Hindu Vidya Niketan, Solar 
installations, supported by UPS systems, have resulted 
in consistent electricity availability, addressing earlier 
disruptions. Science laboratories are fully equipped and in 
use, and LAN systems have been installed to support digital 
infrastructure.
A critical strength is that outputs are not just installed but 
fully integrated into daily school functioning, supported by 
maintenance systems and institutional ownership.
Impact Assessment
At present, the intervention demonstrates strong performance 
at the output level and early adoption stage:
•	 Output Achievement: Complete and functional across all 
components
•	 Usage: High adoption of water and electricity infrastructure
•	 Behavioural Change: Reduced dependence on external 
water sources; improved hygiene practices
•	 Learning Environment: Improved comfort and initial 
academic engagement
Key Quotes
•	 “Complaints regarding water quality have been completely 
resolved.”
•	 “Students now refill water multiple times daily—it has 
become part of their routine.”
•	 “Electricity supply is now consistent, improving 
concentration and comfort.”
•	 “Practical learning has improved as students can now 
experiment in laboratories.”
This intervention represents a strong foundation-stage 
implementation, where the most critical milestone effective 
infrastructure deployment and immediate usability has been 
achieved.
By simultaneously addressing water, energy, academic, and 
digital infrastructure, the project has transformed the schools 
from resource-constrained environments to functionally 
saksham learning spaces.

PROJECT 8 | SCHOOL SANITATION & 
INFRASTRUCTURE PROJECT – CHENNAI
CSR Pillar: Swachh Bharat (Sanitation & Hygiene)
Name of Implementing Partner: Sona Comstar
Project Locations: Chennai
Project Overview
The Sona Comstar Chennai School CSR initiative focuses on 
improving sanitation infrastructure and maintenance of toilet 
systems across 10 government schools in Tamil Nadu. The 
program has evolved from one-time infrastructure creation to 
providing sustained sanitation services, ensuring long-term 
usability of the toilets and behavioural change in school going 
children.
The assessment indicates strong positive outcomes in student 
hygiene, increased utilisation of sanitation facilities, and 
improved school experience, particularly for girls. However, 
challenges remain in infrastructure capacity, sustainability of 
maintenance, and asset management.
Project Objectives
•	 Provide clean sanitation and toilet facilities across 10 
government schools in Tamil Nadu
•	 Construct a dedicated toilet complex for students at Anaikat 
School, Chennai
Project Activities
•	 Maintenance of sanitation infrastructure across 10 
government schools, covering 6670 students
•	 Construction of one restroom complex (separate for boys 
and girls), each measuring 602 sq. ft.
The project demonstrates services across infrastructure 
provision to ensuring that toilet facilities remain functional, 
clean, and usable over time.

Project Outputs
Student Coverage:
•	 Total students impacted: 6,670
•	 Girls: 3,775 (~58%)
•	 Boys: 2,895 (~42%)
This reflects a strong gender-inclusive impact, with more girls 
(58%) as primary beneficiaries, especially due to improved 
sanitation and menstrual hygiene support.
Institutional Coverage:
•	 10 Schools supported (2015–2025)
•	 Long-term engagement (up to 10 years) in early-phase 
schools
•	 Programme expansion in recent years (2023–2025)
Impact Assessment
Based on the secondary data analysis, site visit and 
stakeholder interaction – the following impact was evidenced:
a)	
Access to Sanitation
•	 Availability of high-quality toilet infrastructure with 
water, mirrors, and disposal systems
•	 Facilities comparable to urban/high-standard 
sanitation systems
b)	
Behavioural Change The program has moved beyond 
infrastructure to behavioural transformation, particularly 
among rural students:
•	 Adoption of handwashing and hygiene practices
•	 Improvement in personal grooming habits
•	 Reduction in open defecation practices
•	 Improved menstrual hygiene management
Stakeholder Insights
I.	
School Leadership (Headmasters & Teachers)
	
Sentiment: Highly positive
	
Key Feedback:
•	 Infrastructure rated as “excellent” and “better than 
home facilities”
•	 Significant improvement in student hygiene behaviour
•	 Increased toilet usage and comfort levels
	
Challenges:
•	 Overcrowding, especially among girls (~300+ users)
•	 Lack of school-level funds for future maintenance
II.	
Sanitation Workers
	
Sentiment: Positive, with strong ownership
	
Key Feedback:
•	 Stable and timely income for them (sanitation workers)
•	 Students gradually learning hygiene practices
•	 Pride in maintaining clean facilities
	
Challenges:
•	 High workload during peak periods (break times)
•	 Need for constant supervision to prevent misuse
•	 Balancing cleaning frequency with water/resource 
constraints
III.	
Supervisors / Implementation Team
	
Sentiment: Positive
	
Key Feedback:
•	 Visible improvement in student behaviour over time
•	 Structured maintenance and monitoring systems 
working effectively
	
Challenges:
•	 Asset damage (mirrors, pipes, partitions)
•	 Occasional misuse by students
	
“I am maintaining the girls’ toilets. Students are quite 
helpful and do not cause much trouble. They even pour 
water and keep it clean themselves. They come during 
the interval and sometimes in between, but there are no 
issues with the students. Every time they use it, I clean it. 
We place covers for the disposal of napkins; I clean and 
replace them every day. They do not leave them on top 
of the bin; they put them correctly inside. We then take 
them and burn them with the help of a machine placed 
inside. Yes, we burn them.”
IV.	 Students (Observed Outcomes)
•	 Increased willingness to use toilets
•	 Adoption of hygiene habits
•	 Improved confidence and dignity
•	 Toilets perceived as aspirational spaces
The Sona Comstar Chennai School CSR initiative demonstrates 
a high-impact intervention in school sanitation. By combining 
infrastructure with ongoing maintenance, the program has 
significantly improved student hygiene, dignity, and school 
experience.

PROJECT 9 | PLANTATION AND MAINTENANCE 
OF MIYAWAKI DENSE FOREST OF MULTI SPECIES 
PLANTS UNDER THE “GO GREEN INITIATIVE”
CSR Pillar: Swachh Bharat Programme
Environment & Sustainability
Name of Implementing Partner: Catch Foundation
Project Locations: Manesar, Gurugram
About Implementing Partner
The project is implemented by CATCH Foundation in Manesar, 
Haryana - a semi-arid, ecologically degraded landscape 
characterised by poor soil quality and the presence of 
invasive species. It is anchored within the CSR mandate of 
environmental sustainability and climate resilience, with a 
focus on ecological restoration and water conservation.
The intervention spans in FY 2025-26, approximately 23,000 
saplings at the site level, implemented over a four‑year period 
(one year of plantation followed by maintenance of two years 
from the year of completion).
At the time of assessment, the project is in its early 
establishment phase (Year 0–1). Accordingly, findings should 
be interpreted as output-level achievements and emerging 
early outcomes, rather than mature impact.
The implementing partner, CATCH Foundation, brings 
technical expertise in afforestation, soil rejuvenation, and 
biodiversity conservation, enabling a science-based approach 
to ecological restoration.
Project Overview
The Miyawaki Dense Forest initiative under the “Go Green 
Initiative” is a strategic environmental sustainability 
intervention focused on ecological restoration and climate 
resilience in Manesar, Haryana. Implemented in partnership 
with CATCH Foundation, the project aims to transform 
degraded and semi-arid land into a dense, multi-layered 
green ecosystem using the scientifically driven Miyawaki 
afforestation technique.
The intervention includes the plantation and maintenance of 
over 23,000 native species and bamboo saplings supported 
by soil conditioning, irrigation systems, and long-term 
maintenance mechanisms.
By combining biodiversity restoration with community 
participation and sustainable land management practices, the 
project seeks to improve vegetation cover, enhance ecological 
balance, and contribute to long-term climate resilience. The 
initiative represents Sona Comstar’s commitment toward 
environmental stewardship and the creation of sustainable 
green infrastructure.
Project Objectives
The project aims to establish a dense, multi-species 
plantation that gradually restores ecological balance in 
degraded landscapes.
In the immediate term, the focus is on ensuring sapling survival 
and soil conditioning. Over the medium term, the intervention 
seeks to enable biodiversity regeneration and improve micro-
ecological conditions, including soil moisture and vegetation 
density.
In the long term, the project aspires to contribute to 
groundwater recharge, climate resilience, and the 
development of a self-sustaining forest ecosystem, supported 
by community stewardship.
Project Activities
The intervention applies the Miyawaki afforestation method, 
characterised by high-density plantation (2–4 saplings per 
square meter) and integrated water management systems.
A defining feature of the project is its scientifically structured, 
multi-layer plantation design, which replicates natural forest 
ecosystems. The plantation integrates a diverse mix of 
native species and bamboo across vertical layers to enhance 
ecological functionality.
•	 Canopy layer: Includes long-lived species such as Neem, 
Banyan (Bargad), Peepal, Mango, Kadam, and Shisham, 
which provide structural stability, shade, and long-term 
microclimate regulation.
•	 Tree layer: Comprises species such as Jamun, Arjun, Amla, 
Kathal, Chilbil, and Mahogany, which support biodiversity 
by providing food sources and habitats for birds and 
insects.
•	 Sub-tree and shrub layer: Includes species such as Lemon, 
Ber, Kadhi Patta, Ashoka, Desi Anar, and Guava (Peru), 
contributing to ground-level density, soil enrichment, and 
moisture retention.
The plantation follows a dual-species composition, with 
approximately 10,000 Bambusa balcooa (Beema bamboo) 
and 13,000 native species. This mix balances rapid biomass 
growth with ecological resilience and biodiversity support.
The intervention also contributes to a broader “Green Campus” 
transformation, converting degraded land into a structured 
ecological asset that supports environmental regeneration 
and institutional sustainability.
Implementation followed a technically sequenced and 
process-driven approach, ensuring long-term ecological 
viability.
Initial activities included baseline surveys, geological 
assessments, and soil testing, enabling site-specific plantation 
strategies. Land preparation involved the removal of invasive 
species (such as Vilayati Kikar), followed by deep excavation 
(up to 3 feet) to improve soil aeration and root penetration. Soil 
enrichment was carried out using organic inputs, including 
manure, compost, and vermicompost.
To ensure water availability, borewell infrastructure (~200 ft 
depth) was established, supported by drip irrigation systems 
designed for efficient water use. Plantation was carried out 
using high-density spacing, followed by fencing and protective 
measures.
Post-plantation maintenance includes regular irrigation, pest 
and termite management, soil conditioning, and continuous 
monitoring through supervisors and standard operating 
procedures.
Project Outputs
Physically, over 23,000 saplings have been planted, supported 
by irrigation infrastructure and protective systems.
Operationally, the project has established a structured 
implementation and monitoring framework, with 12–16 
workers engaged per month, contributing to local employment 
generation.
Ecologically, the primary output is the conversion of degraded 
land into a structured, multi-layer vegetation system, 
supported by improved soil conditions and moisture retention.
At this stage, the project is transitioning from outputs to early 
outcomes, with sapling establishment and survival serving as 
key indicators.
Impact Assessment
At the current stage, impact remains emergent and not fully 
measurable.
While the project demonstrates strong implementation 
performance, key outcome indicators such as sapling survival 
rates, biodiversity restoration, groundwater recharge, and 
carbon sequestration are under validation.
A significant outcome to date is the transformation of barren, 
degraded land into a structured plantation ecosystem.
This transformation has resulted in:
•	 Increased vegetation cover
•	 Reduced exposed soil surfaces
•	 Early signs of ecological activity, including the presence of 
birds and insects
These changes represent initial ecological recovery signals.
Carbon Sequestration Potential – Plantation Initiative
This project is in its second year. Preliminary carbon 
sequestration estimates have been calculated for the planting 
done in Site 1 – planted in FY 2024-2025 and Site 2 planted 
in FY 2024-2025
The estimates are indicative and represent potential future 
carbon sequestration capacity. Since the plantations are still 
in the early growth stage, the plants are presently too young to 
achieve mature carbon absorption levels. The calculations are 
based on an estimated survival rate of approximately derived 
from the surviving plant counts observed during the assessment.

Under the Miyawaki Intervention
In FY 2024–25, over 45,767 saplings were planted using 
dense multi-layer Miyawaki plantation methods, including:
•	 Approximately 20,000 Beema bamboo saplings and 25767 
native tree species;
•	 Establishment of irrigation, soil conditioning, and ecological 
restoration systems;
•	 Estimated plantation survival rates of approximately 90% 
for native species and 93% for bamboo plantations;
In FY 2025–26, over 23,000 saplings were planted using 
dense multi-layer Miyawaki plantation methods, including:
•	 Approximately 10,000 Beema bamboo saplings and 
13,000 native tree species;
•	 Establishment of irrigation, soil conditioning, and ecological 
restoration systems;
•	 Estimated plantation survival rates of approximately 99% 
for native species and 94.4% for bamboo plantations;
The bamboo plantations were estimated separately, 
considering that Bambusa balcooa generally demonstrates 
significantly higher carbon absorption potential compared to 
conventional native species plantations. 
Given the early stage of plantation maturity, continued 
maintenance, monitoring, watering support, and survival 
tracking will be critical to ensuring that the projected 
environmental outcomes are realised over the coming years.
FGD - Based Behavioural Insights
FGD and KII findings indicate early-stage behavioural and 
perception shifts.
There is a clear transition in how the land is perceived 
from barren and underutilised to a valuable ecological 
asset. Participation in plantation activities has increased 
awareness of environmental practices and enabled hands-
on engagement with ecological processes.
A gradual shift is observed from labour participation to 
emerging stewardship, with workers increasingly involved in 
monitoring plant health and maintenance.
